diagrams.title: DAC Architecture Block Diagram
diagrams.description: A comprehensive layered architecture showing the internal structure of a Decentralized Autonomous Corporation, including smart contracts, token layer, governance layer, and stakeholder interactions
elements: Top Layer: External Interfaces (DApps, Wallets, API)
Governance Layer: Voting Mechanism, Proposal System, Policy Engine
Smart Contract Layer: Core Contracts (Token, Treasury, Operations, Identity)
Data Layer: Blockchain Ledger, Oracle Data, IPFS Storage
Token Economy: Utility Tokens, Governance Tokens, Revenue Distribution
Stakeholder Nodes: Token Holders, Service Providers, Users, Investors
Arrows showing data flow between layers and stakeholders
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the multi-layered architecture of DACs and how different components interact to create a self-governing corporate entity

diagrams.title: Traditional Corporation vs DAC Structure Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side structural comparison showing hierarchical vs decentralized organizational patterns
elements: Left Side - Traditional: CEO → Board → Managers → Employees → Customers (vertical hierarchy)
Right Side - DAC: Distributed Network → Smart Contracts → Token Holders → Service Providers → Community (horizontal network)
Arrows showing command flow: Traditional shows top-down, DAC shows peer-to-peer
Key differences highlighted: Centralized Authority vs Distributed Consensus
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize the fundamental structural difference between traditional corporations and DACs, understanding decentralization as an organizational paradigm shift

diagrams.title: DAC Operational Flow Diagram
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flowchart showing how a typical DAC operation (proposal to execution) flows through the system
elements: Start: Community Member Proposal
Step 1: Smart Contract receives and validates proposal
Step 2: Voting period initiated, token-weighted voting
Step 3: Vote counting and threshold check
Step 4a: If approved → Execute smart contract function
Step 4b: If rejected → Archive proposal
Step 5: Results recorded on blockchain
Step 6: Treasury distribution (if applicable)
Arrows with decision diamonds and process boxes
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete lifecycle of decentralized decision-making in a DAC, from proposal initiation to execution

diagrams.title: DAC Governance and Tokenomics Model
diagrams.description: Diagram showing the economic incentives and governance mechanisms that sustain a DAC
elements: Token Distribution Circle: Pre-mined tokens, Rewards, Staking
Governance Rights: Voting Power, Proposal Rights, Veto Power
Incentive Mechanisms: Staking Rewards, Fee Distribution, Profit Sharing
Treasury System: Revenue Collection → Allocation → Distribution
Value Creation: Services/Products → Revenue → Token Value → Rewards
Feedback loops showing token value influencing governance participation
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how economic incentives align stakeholder interests and create sustainable governance in DACs

diagrams.title: Real-World DAC Examples Classification
diagrams.description: Classification diagram showing different types of DACs with representative examples
elements: DeFi DACs: MakerDAO, Uniswap (financial services)
Social Media DACs: Steem, Lens Protocol (content platforms)
Gaming DACs: Axie Infinity, Enjin (gaming ecosystems)
Infrastructure DACs: Filecoin, The Graph (decentralized services)
Investment DACs: BitDAO, The LAO (collective investment)
Each category showing: Purpose, Token, Governance Model, Key Feature
diagrams.purpose: Students see practical applications of DACs across different industries and understand the versatility of the model

charts.title: Traditional Corporation vs DAC Comparison Table
charts.content: Detailed comparison covering: Authority Structure (Centralized vs Distributed), Decision Making (Board/Management vs Token-weighted Voting), Transparency (Limited vs Full On-chain), Operating Hours (Business Hours vs 24/7), Costs (High overhead vs Low automation), Scalability (Hierarchical teams vs Programmatic scaling), Legal Status (Registered entity vs Often undefined), Profit Distribution (Shareholder dividends vs Token appreciation/rewards), Changes/Upgrades (Slow formal process vs Fast protocol updates), Exit/Exit (Merger/Acquisition difficult vs Fork possibility)

charts.title: DAC Creation and Lifecycle Process
charts.content: Flowchart showing stages: 1) Founding Team creates whitepaper and tokenomics → 2) Smart contracts developed and audited → 3) Token generation event (TGE) → 4) Community building and token distribution → 5) Governance activation → 6) Proposal submission begins → 7) Iterative improvement through DAO votes → 8) Scale or fork based on community decisions

charts.title: DAC vs DAO vs Traditional Corporate Structures
charts.content: Three-column comparison: DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization - general purpose, flexible governance, focus on community), DAC (Decentralized Autonomous Corporation - profit-oriented, business model focus, revenue generation), Traditional Corporation (Centralized, profit maximization, legal compliance). Include: Primary Goal, Governance Token Purpose, Revenue Model, Legal Framework, Decision Speed, Member vs Shareholder

charts.title: DAC Key Components and Functions
charts.content: Table with columns: Component (Governance Token, Smart Contracts, Treasury, Voting Mechanism, Oracle, Proposal System) and Function (Representation of value and voting rights, Automated execution and rules, Fund management and distribution, Collective decision-making, External data integration, Idea submission and filtering). Include specific examples for each: ERC-20/ERC-721 tokens, Solidity contracts, Multi-sig wallets, Quadratic voting, Chainlink, Snapshot/IPFS

branches: Core Principles: Autonomous, Decentralized, Profit-oriented, Code-as-law, Token-incentivized
Architecture: Smart Contracts, Blockchain, Oracles, Token Layer, Governance Layer, Interfaces
Governance: Token-weighted voting, Proposal system, Quorum requirements, Representation models
Economics: Tokenomics, Treasury management, Revenue distribution, Staking rewards, Fee mechanisms
Advantages: 24/7 operation, Global accessibility, Transparency, Reduced corruption, Lower costs, Fast decisions
Challenges: Legal uncertainty, Technical risks, Oracle dependency, Voter apathy, Fork risks, Scalability
Real Examples: MakerDAO (DeFi), BitDAO (Investment), Dash (Payments), Brave (Browser), Filecoin (Storage)
Lifecycle: Whitepaper → Development → TGE → Governance Launch → Operations → Scaling/Forking
vs Other Models: Compared to Traditional Corp (centralized), DAO (mission-driven), DAO LLC (hybrid legal)
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of DACs - from technical architecture to economic models, governance mechanisms, and real-world applications - helping students see the big picture of corporate decentralization

branches: Participants: Token Holders (vote), Developers (build), Service Providers (deliver), Users (consume), Investors (fund)
Technology Stack: Blockchain (Ethereum, Solana), Smart Contracts, Oracles (Chainlink), Storage (IPFS), Frontend (React)
Business Functions: Fundraising (token sale), Operations (automated), Revenue (fees/services), Distribution (treasury)
Legal & Regulatory: Token classification (security/utility), Tax implications, Jurisdictional challenges, Compliance attempts
Success Metrics: Token holder count, Proposal volume, Treasury value, User adoption, Governance participation rate
Failure Modes: Hack/exploit, Governance capture, Low participation, Regulatory action, Technical obsolescence
mindMaps.purpose: Holistic view of the complete DAC ecosystem showing interconnections between participants, technology, business, and regulatory aspects - essential for understanding practical implementation challenges
